---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## Compiling: Overleaf and the Local Build

- Part 3 — compile the paper, on Overleaf or locally

::: notes
Welcome back to Lab Eight, part three of twelve. In part two we built a minimal-but-complete main-dot-tex that reads its results instead of containing them. Now we turn it into a PDF, two ways: the Overleaf browser workflow that needs nothing installed, and the local latexmk or tectonic build on the camp container.
:::

## The Overleaf Workflow

- Overleaf is a browser LaTeX editor — nothing to install
- First: upload `paper/`, preserving the folder structure
- Second: set main document and compiler to pdfLaTeX
- Third: click *Recompile* — PDF renders in the right pane
- Overleaf reruns passes to resolve refs and cites

::: notes
Overleaf is a browser LaTeX editor; it needs nothing on your machine. The workflow is three moves. First, create a new project and upload your paper folder — main-dot-tex, the tables folder, and the figures folder — preserving the structure, because Overleaf keeps subfolders and your input and graphics-path lines depend on them. Second, set the main document to main-dot-tex and the compiler to pdf-LaTeX, under Menu, Settings, Compiler — or plain LaTeX if you later add BibTeX. Third, click Recompile. The PDF renders in the right pane, and Overleaf reruns the passes needed to resolve your references and citations automatically. Three moves, no install.
:::

## Overleaf Is a Compiler, Not an Author

- Upload the tables and figures your code just generated
- Never files you edited in the browser
- Regenerate locally, then upload
- Edit a number in Overleaf and you break the chain
- That reintroduces the exact drift the packet prevents

::: notes
The discipline that matters on Overleaf is the same one that matters everywhere: the tables and figures you upload must be the ones your code just generated, not files you edited in the browser. The honest move is to regenerate them locally, or on the container, with your analysis, and then upload. If you ever edit a number in Overleaf, you have broken the chain and reintroduced the exact drift the whole packet exists to prevent. Overleaf is a compiler, not a place to author results. Keep authoring in your code and let Overleaf only typeset.
:::

## The Local latexmk Path

- On the container you compile in place
- `latexmk -pdf -interaction=nonstopmode main.tex`
- It figures out how many passes are needed and runs them
- `-pdf` selects PDF output via pdflatex
- `nonstopmode` keeps a warning from halting an unattended build

::: notes
On the camp container, you skip the upload entirely and compile in place. The simplest robust command is latexmk, which figures out how many passes are needed to resolve cross-references and citations and runs them for you. You call latexmk, dash-pdf, dash-interaction-nonstopmode, main-dot-tex. The dash-pdf flag selects PDF output via pdflatex under the hood. The nonstopmode flag means a recoverable warning does not stop the build to wait for keyboard input — which is important when this runs unattended inside make. The result is paper-slash-main-dot-pdf.
:::

## tectonic, Cleaning, and Reading Errors

- `tectonic main.tex` — self-contained, downloads packages
- `latexmk -C` deletes every auxiliary file and the PDF
- A first run may rerun to resolve references — not an error
- A genuinely broken build exits non-zero
- Grep the log: `grep -nE "^!|Error" main.log`

::: notes
If you prefer the self-contained engine, tectonic needs no separate driver and downloads any missing packages on first use — you just run tectonic main-dot-tex. To start clean, which you will do constantly while debugging, latexmk dash-capital-C deletes every auxiliary file — the aux, log, f-l-s, f-d-b, and out files — and the PDF, so the next build is from nothing. A note from verifying this lab: a first latexmk run on a fresh paper may print "LaTeX failed to resolve N references" and rerun automatically. That is latexmk doing its job across passes, not an error, and the final exit code is what you check. A genuinely broken build — a missing input file, an undefined control sequence, the natbib clash — exits non-zero and names the offending line in main-dot-log. Grep it with grep, dash-n, dash-capital-E, caret-exclamation or Error, main-dot-log.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Two roads to the same PDF: **Overleaf** (upload `paper/`, set pdfLaTeX, Recompile) needs no install; **`latexmk -pdf`** or **`tectonic`** compiles on the container. Both are compilers — upload only code-generated tables and figures. Next: finalizing the repo as a one-click packet.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part three: there are two roads to the same PDF. Overleaf needs no install — upload the paper folder preserving structure, set the compiler to pdf-LaTeX, and click Recompile. Locally on the container, latexmk dash-pdf resolves passes for you, and tectonic does it self-contained. Either way, both are only compilers: upload and compile the tables and figures your code generated, never numbers you edited by hand. And read latexmk's automatic reruns as normal, not errors — check the exit code and grep the log. In part four we finalize the repository as a one-click packet, starting with the one seed and the code in run-order.
:::
